data.title: From Gaming To Web Advertising, AppLovin's New Focus After Major Divestment
data.description:
I last wrote about AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) on February 22, 2025, when I assigned a buy rating to the stock, which was trading at $411.86. It has not fared well since then. The stock hit an intraday low of $200.50 on April 7, 2025, due to a general sell-off of tech stocks and several short sellers writing reports against the company. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Adam Foroughi briefly acknowledged short seller activity on the first quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 earnings call:

After seeing the stock price rise roughly 50 times in two years, we faced short seller scrutiny, which we've addressed comprehensively and won't revisit here. Our mission remains clear, helping advertisers reach new customers profitably.

For investors interested in the matter, CEO Foroughi provided his complete response to short sellers in a CEO Letter dated February 26, 2025. The company has also retained legal counsel to investigate the allegations in one of the short reports. Despite the potentially damaging allegations, the market remains interested in this company. The stock price has recovered from its lows to reach a closing price of $354.29 on May 23. Much of the stock recovery was due to the company issuing an excellent first-quarter fiscal year FY 2025 report on May 7, with earnings and revenue that exceeded Wall Street expectations, leading to a surge in the stock price. However, it is still down 10.17% from where I recommended it on February 22.

Still, my previous buy recommendation shouldn't remain underwater for long. Management is doubling down on its high-margin advertising business by selling its lower-margin games business to Tripledot Studios. A Reuters press release on May 8 stated, "London-based mobile game developer Tripledot Studios has agreed to buy marketing platform AppLovin's mobile games studio portfolio for about $800 million in cash and stock, it said on Thursday." This move allows AppLovin to concentrate its investments fully on its high-growth, high-margin advertising platform. The advertising business reported an impressive 81% adjusted EBITDA margin in its first quarter of FY 2025.

The core of AppLovin's success is its proprietary AI engine, Axon. CEO Adam Foroughi said on the company's first quarter FY 2025 earnings call:

We're relentlessly improving our machine learning models. Our research science team is leveraging rapid AI advancements to deliver even greater value to our partners, ensuring our platform remains a performance leader.

Its technological edge over competing platforms has already driven significant growth in the gaming app ecosystem, and the company expects to continue this trend. The company believes its marketing platform has allowed gaming app manufacturers to succeed over the last several years. CEO Foroughi said on the earnings call, "Our technological innovations have catalyzed the return to growth in the gaming ecosystem, reviving an industry that would otherwise be struggling without our advancements over the past two years."

AppLovin is now leveraging the marketing engine it developed for gaming and applying it to other areas, specifically e-commerce and web advertising. The company expects this move to significantly expand its total addressable market [TAM].

This article will discuss where AppLovin will prioritize its investments after the divestment of its gaming business. It will also examine its first quarter FY 2025 earnings report, risks, valuation, and why I still recommend a buy.

AppLovin Investment Priorities

CEO Foroughi highlighted three key investment priorities during the company's first-quarter earnings call. The first investing priority he discussed was continuously improving its core AI ("AXON") to deliver even better ad performance, measured by revenue and profitability, for its advertising partners. The company defines AXON on its website:

AXON AI, our AI-powered advertising engine powers AppDiscovery, the cornerstone of our advertising solutions. It enables the matching of advertiser demand with publisher supply through auctions at vast scale and at microsecond-level speeds, helping advertisers maximize the return on their advertising spend.

AppLovin transitioned from AXON 1.0 to AXON 2.0 in early 2023. AXON 2.0, compared to AXON's previous generation, shows increased predictive accuracy in identifying users most likely to download or engage with the advertised product or application and helps predict which ads will generate the highest return on ad spending (ROAS) for advertisers. Following the introduction of Axon 2, Northbeam, a marketing intelligence platform, stated that "AppLovin demonstrates a 45% higher ROAS compared to Meta." Additionally, it displayed data showing that Axon outperforms TikTok, Pinterest (PINS), Snap Inc. (SNAP), and YouTube in generating ROAS for advertisers. Thus, AXON 2.0 differentiates AppLovin from competing ad platforms and has become a competitive advantage.

When comparing AXON 2.0 to another staunch competitor, The Trade Desk, with its Koa AI, it compares favorably and perhaps has an advantage because of its better ease of use. The Trade Desk has positioned Koa as a co-pilot software for marketers, enabling advertisers to customize their ad campaigns in-depth. In contrast, AppLovin operates more like black box automation, where advertisers provide basic inputs such as budget, geography, and desired ROAS, and AXON 2.0's automation "does the rest of the work" to identify users most likely to engage with the ad. It's hands-off automation. Both Koa and AXON 2.0 are powerful AI applications that will attract different types of users. AXON 2.0 will attract advertisers who want an easier-to-use platform with automated, hands-off optimization. Koa will attract large enterprises that want to run highly granular, sophisticated ad campaigns and who can afford to hire the talent to run The Trade Desk's sophisticated software.

Gaming industry consulting firm Navaak wrote on April 3, 2024:

In Q1 2023, AppLovin launched AXON 2.0, which was more efficient than its predecessor and drove four consecutive quarters of double-digit quarter-over-quarter growth of its AppDiscovery product. The software business ended the year up 46%, which is particularly impressive given the challenging economic backdrop and another stagnant year in mobile gaming.

AppLovin's revenue growth substantially improved after the introduction of AXON 2.0. Competitors have noticed and are actively attempting to catch up. However, AppLovin's research science team remains at the forefront of AI research and quickly integrates new findings into its AXON platform, ensuring it adopts cutting-edge techniques before competitors can. Its continued investment in AXON makes its technology a fast-moving target, making it harder for competitors to replicate. The company employs a large number of PhD researchers who are constantly working on new versions and helping maintain its competitive edge.

The company's second priority is advancing e-commerce and web advertising solutions, with three specific areas of focus:

  • Enhancing its AI effectiveness and performance for non-gaming web advertisers.
  • Making it easier for web advertisers to connect AppLovin's existing tools and measure ad campaign performance accurately.
  • Developing and introducing a self-service dashboard to automate the setup and management of ad campaigns for new advertisers.

The third priority is improving the creative side of advertising. The company will utilize generative AI to enable advertisers to create, test, and improve ad creatives more effectively. CEO Adam Foroughi discussed ad creatives on the earnings call:

In the world of large language models and customization, we're not going to get to the extreme where every single end user can see a differentiated video, but we can certainly get to a place where you can take the best videos that an advertiser uploads, run them through a large language model, get an output of more videos that are dynamically generated, run those videos through our platform, let the model then personalize the ad to the end user at a greater level. When you're able to test ad creative, it's one of the biggest levers that advertisers have to move the needle on growth, on response rate. It's free effectively. It's a very low cost amount of production to get a much higher response rate on advertising. If we can systematically do it, and we're certain we can, it's just taking time to get this to market, we're going to be able to extract a lot higher response rate from the audience that sees the advertisements.

The hope is that its ad creatives will yield higher response rates and enhanced ad campaign effectiveness.

Company Fundamentals

Let's dive into our outstanding Q1 performance. The first quarter is typically challenging for advertising due to seasonality and fewer days compared to Q4, yet we achieved remarkable growth. How? We further refined our machine learning models enabling mobile gaming companies to scale their campaigns on our platform. Less significant, but impactful was a full quarter contribution from web advertisers. This diversification is transformative and fuels our excitement for what's ahead.

Source: CEO Adam Foroughi on First Quarter 2025 Earnings Call.

AppLovin's first quarter FY 2025 advertising revenue increased 71% to $1.159 billion. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Matt Stumpf said about the advertising business on the earnings call:

Our revenue growth in the quarter was driven by a combination of factors, including continued enhancements in our AI-driven technology, which has delivered even better performance for the advertisers using app discovery and the full quarter impact of our web-based advertising solution, which continues to perform strongly while coming off a seasonally high spend period for e-commerce.

The advertising business is not only growing very rapidly, but it is also highly profitable. Its advertising business's first-quarter adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) increased 92% year-over-year to $943 million, and its first-quarter FY 2025 adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by eight points over the previous year's comparable quarter to 81%.

CFO Stumpf said on the first quarter earnings call:

Quarter-over-quarter flow-through from revenue to adjusted EBITDA was an exceptional 104%, which is slightly higher than our normal levels due to certain non-recurring costs last quarter. However, after adjusting for these costs, our flow-through was still a robust 100% despite the step-up in data center costs I mentioned last quarter, highlighting our dedication to operating lean while scaling smartly.

Flow-through from revenue to adjusted EBITDA measures the percentage of additional revenue from one time period to the other that is passed through to adjusted EBITDA. For instance, if a company generates an additional $100 in revenue and its Adjusted EBITDA increases by $100, that represents 100% flow-through, implying that the company didn't incur any additional variable costs when generating the additional revenue or that any extra costs were offset by extreme efficiency elsewhere. A sequential flow-through of revenue to adjusted EBITDA of 100% is a sign of a highly scalable business, meaning it's a business that can grow significantly and generate substantial profits without its expenses skyrocketing.

AppLovin's first quarter FY 2025 total revenue increased 40% year-over-year to $1.484 billion, beating analysts' estimates by $101.76 million.

AppLovin's first-quarter FY 2025 net income increased 144% to $576 million, and the net income margin expanded by 17 points to 39%. Its first-quarter GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) diluted earnings per share ("EPS") increased by 149% to $1.67, beating analysts' estimates by $0.23.

The company's first-quarter earnings release defines adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EBITDA margin:

We define Adjusted EBITDA for a particular period as net income before interest expense and loss on settlement of debt, other income, net (excluding certain recurring items), provision for (benefit from) income taxes, amortization, depreciation and write-offs and as further adjusted for non-operating foreign exchange (gains) losses, stock-based compensation expense, transaction-related expense, goodwill impairment, restructuring costs, loss on disposal of long-lived assets, as well as certain other items that we believe are not reflective of our core operating performance. We define Adjusted EBITDA margin as Adjusted EBITDA divided by revenue for the same period.

The company's total adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter of fiscal year 2025 increased 83% to $1.005 billion, and its adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by 16 points to 68%.

AppLovin's first quarter FY 2025 cash from operations ("CFO") increased 112% year over year to $832 million, and its free cash flow ("FCF") increased 113% to $826 million.

Its first-quarter FY 2025 trailing 12-month CFO to sales ratio is 49.42%, meaning that for every $1 of sales over the last year, it produced $0.49.

The company's first-quarter FY 2025 TTM CFO and FCF are the same at $2.538 billion, indicating that AppLovin barely incurs capital expenditures.

The company used some of that FCF to reduce its share count. Chief Financial Officer Stumpf said on the first quarter earnings call:

This quarter, we repurchased and withheld a total of 3.4 million shares for a total cost of $1.2 billion, primarily funded through our free cash flow as well as a temporary draw on our revolving credit facility, which we've now repaid. As a result of our strategic share management activities, we were able to reduce the total outstanding shares, net of share issuances to employees, demonstrating our commitment to delivering value to shareholders, and ended the quarter with 338 million shares outstanding.

AppLovin's cash and equivalents total $551.024 million, and its long-term debt amounts to $3.51 billion. The company's debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio is 6.45, indicating that the company finances its operations primarily with debt rather than equity. The market often associates this level of D/E with increased risk. It has a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.24, suggesting that the company could theoretically pay off all its debt in 1.24 years or less by dedicating all its EBITDA to debt repayment. The company's current ratio of 1.68 indicates that it has sufficient liquid assets to meet its near-term obligations, which are due within a year.

Fitch published its debt rating on AppLovin on November 1, 2024:

Fitch has also assigned a 'BBB-' rating to AppLovin's first lien secured revolver and term loans maturing 2028 and 2030, respectively. The rating reflects AppLovin's leading market position among performance marketing ad-tech companies focused on mobile gaming. Recent model enhancements have significantly boosted the company's operational metrics, with revenue and EBITDA growing in double digits leading to improved scale, strong FCF generation and enhanced financial flexibility. Additionally, Fitch expects AppLovin to maintain a conservative financial policy, with EBITDA leverage below 2.0x.

A Fitch bond rated BBB- is a lower medium investment grade, indicating that AppLovin has sufficient capacity to meet its financial commitments. However, adverse economic conditions or changing circumstances increase the likelihood that the company's capacity to meet its financial obligations will weaken.

Since the company sold its Apps business, it only provided second-quarter FY 2025 guidance for its Advertising segment. Management forecasted total advertising revenue of between $1.195 and $1.215 billion. This number was below analysts' second-quarter FY 2025 advertising revenue estimates of $1.38 billion. If the company reaches the midpoint of its second quarter advertising revenue guidance ($1.205 billion), it will have increased by 69% year-over-year.

Management also forecast second-quarter FY advertising adjusted EBITDA of between $970 million and $990 million. If the company reaches the midpoint of its second-quarter advertising-adjusted EBITDA estimates ($980 million), it will grow 88% year-over-year. If the company achieves its forecasted second-quarter FY 2025 advertising-adjusted EBITDA margin of 81%, its adjusted EBITDA margin will expand by eight points year over year.

The market generally approved of the first quarter results. The stock rose by around 12% the day after the company released its first-quarter FY 2025 earnings report.

Risks

Although management has actively worked to diversify its business, over 90% of AppLovin's advertising revenue continues to come from mobile games. If a significant downturn or disruption were to occur in the mobile gaming industry, it could adversely impact its primary revenue stream. The market would likely respond by selling off the stock.

Although new verticals like web advertising and e-commerce have high upside revenue potential, the company faces execution risks by expanding into these areas. Currently, AppLovin has only a small team working on new verticals and has yet to fully build the ad tools and integrate third-party measurement and analytics tools with AppLovin's Axon. The company also needs to integrate its platform with other software systems and services that advertisers commonly use in their daily operations. When an analyst asked in the first quarter FY 2025 earnings call about the "pain points" that advertisers want to see AppLovin solve, CEO Adam Foroughi said:

What we're excited about today is the model is still in infancy when it comes to web advertising. We think it can get a lot better. And then, you layer on those attribution integrations and platform integrations and make the integration with advertisers more seamless. Both those two things, the automated tools, better integrations, paired with a much better model, which our model is inevitably going to get better as the engineering team has more time to evolve it, we think we're going to get to a place in web advertising where we are in games.

Currently, AppLovin has a manual onboarding process for large advertisers outside of the traditional gaming sector, which limits its growth in other verticals. AppLovin needs to introduce an automated onboarding process for new advertisers to get started, eliminating the need for extensive manual support and making the platform easier and faster for new advertisers to use. Developing the automated tools and better integrations will take time and carry execution risk. If the company struggles to build the necessary technology and fails to attract and retain non-gaming advertisers, it may fail to achieve its long-term revenue growth targets.

The digital advertising space is highly competitive. It faces established giants in the space, such as Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), The Trade Desk (TTD), Unity Software (U), and Meta Platforms (META). It also faces aggressive private digital advertising companies, such as Moloco, InMobi, and StartApp. Although AppLovin management appears confident in its AI expertise and data moat, some of its competitors have more resources and brand recognition. AppLovin will need to continuously invest in research and development to differentiate its solutions from those of its competitors, which are also investing heavily in their platforms. In response to competition, CEO Foroughi said the following on the first quarter earnings call:

On competition, we embrace it. It drives innovation and pushes us all to improve. With our growing data moat and AI expertise, we're confident in our leadership and ability to grow rapidly regardless of others' advancements.

A "data moat" is a competitive advantage that a company gains over time by collecting, analyzing, and using proprietary data that competitors cannot easily replicate.

Valuation

AppLovin's TTM price-to-sales (P/S) ratio of 23.93 is significantly above its three-year median of 5.029, indicating potential overvaluation. However, investors should be cautious about relying solely on backward-looking valuations, such as the trailing twelve-month [TTM] price-to-sales (P/S) ratio, to determine a stock's value. If the company grows its sales faster than its price increases, the TTM P/S ratio can decline toward its median without the stock price declining. Depending on the company's future sales growth, the market may undervalue AppLovin in hindsight.

Based on 2025 forward revenue growth, TTM FCF, TTM profit margin, and TTM ROIC (Return On Invested Capital), AppLovin outpaces The Trade Desk, Zeta Global (ZETA), Magnite (MGNI), and Alphabet, which supports a premium P/S-to-revenue growth (PS/G) ratio of 0.88. Based solely on the numbers below, the market is likely to be more willing to pay a premium for AppLovin, given its robust profitability and future revenue growth potential compared to competitors.

Company Name 2025 forward P/S ratio 2025 forward estimated revenue growth (PS/G) 2025 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth FCF margin TTM operating margin TTM ROIC
AppLovin 20.61 23.54% 0.88 49.26% 46.47% 43.73%
Zeta Global 2.48 23.37% 0.11 9.74% -3.42 -8.11
The Trade Desk 12.69 17.02% 0.75 26.68% 17.63% 16.02%
Magnite 3.30 8.22% 0.40 36% 9.42% 2.41%
Alphabet 5.27 10.74% 0.49 20.81% 32.67% 33.93%

AppLovin's TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 64.05, which is significantly above its Information Technology ("IT") sector median of 26.85, suggests overvaluation. Its forward P/E of 44.61 is above its IT sector median of 27.26, which also suggests overvaluation.

However, AppLovin's forward P/E to growth ("PEG") ratio for FY 2025 is 0.59, calculated by dividing the forward P/E of 44.61 by the analysts' estimated EPS growth rate of 75.30%. Generally, the market considers companies with a PEG ratio below 1.0 as undervalued and those with a PEG ratio above 2.0 as overvalued. Suppose the stock sold at a FY 2025 PEG ratio of 1.0; its price would be $597.88, a 69% rise over its May 23 closing price of $354.29.

AppLovin's forward PEG ratio for FY 2025 is 0.75. Suppose the stock sold at an FY 2026 PEG ratio of 1.0; its price would be $472.32, a 33% increase over its May 23 closing price of $354.29.

AppLovin's TTM price-to-FCF (P/FCF) ratio of 48.42 is significantly above its three-year median of 21.04, indicating potential overvaluation. However, this metric is also backward-looking. If the company successfully transitions its ad platform into new verticals, its FCF will likely grow rapidly over the next several years, aligning its P/FCF valuation with its median without a significant stock decline. So, the marketplace may not necessarily overvalue it by P/FCF.

AppLovin Is A Buy

The company's web advertising pilot currently has approximately 600 advertisers at a $1 billion run rate, according to AppLovin's CEO; web advertising penetration, according to CEO Foroughi, is "less than 0.1% of the total potential market for advertisers." So, website advertising has a high potential upside. When management starts rolling out some of its tools in pilot mode to the full market, such as the new self-service dashboard, the company can expect to see a noticeable increase in revenue growth.

Investors seeking high potential upside in AI-powered advertising technology should consider AppLovin. The company's 2025 forward PEG ratio suggests that the market undervalues the stock by 69% compared to the company's closing price on May 23. I rate the stock a buy.

data.title: PagerDuty: I See Operational Progress, But Growth Needs A Second Act
data.description:
Thesis

PagerDuty, Inc. (NYSE:PD), the incident-response SaaS company that promises to keep digital systems from imploding, has carved out a spot in the automation and IT firefighting business. Its platform plugs into cloud services and third-party tools to help teams catch problems before they spiral. In this analysis, I argue that PagerDuty is at a strategic turning point. In other words, while the company is making real progress internally, it won’t achieve lasting success unless it can also reignite revenue growth, strengthen its sales execution, and outpace growing competition.

PagerDuty’s Q1 2026 Highlights

PagerDuty reported Q1 2026 non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.24, a clean $0.05 above Wall Street estimates. Its non-GAAP operating margin hit 20%, a full 500 basis points above internal targets and a decent jump from the 15% posted in the same quarter last year. That shift in profitability, especially in a sector where many firms are still hemorrhaging cash in pursuit of growth, signals a kind of strategic maturity.

Moving on, margin growth came on the back of a 60% increase in operating income, which jumped from $15 million to $24 million year-over-year. Meanwhile, free cash flow reached $29 million, which represented 24% of total revenue, putting PagerDuty well within elite SaaS territory in terms of efficiency. PD also maintained a gross margin of 86%, landing at the very top of its internal range, while overall revenue rose 7.77% to $119.81 million, beating consensus estimates by $828,000.

These metrics point to a company that is beginning to translate operational improvements into sustainable financial outcomes. Still, strong financial results only go so far without understanding the underlying drivers. In PagerDuty’s case, those results appear to reflect broader shifts in how enterprises are adopting AI and automation, specifically, a growing demand for tools that not only help manage incidents but increasingly aim to prevent them.

Nowhere is this clearer than in PagerDuty’s recurring revenue engine. Annual recurring revenue (ARR) reached $496 million, up 7% from a year ago. Trailing 12-month billings came in at $492 million, also up 7%, a reliable proxy for future revenue recognition. And critically, the dollar-based net retention rate clocked in at 104%, with 25% of enterprise customers expanding their usage of the platform in the last quarter alone.

This stickiness is evident in the company’s customer base, which saw the largest quarterly net addition in two years, with 127 new paid customers, bringing the total to 15,247. Broader usage spans over 32,000 companies, a 9% increase year-over-year, and international revenue surged 11%, now comprising 28% of total revenue.

That brings us to the platform. Its modular platform now supports over 750 third-party connectors, including integrations with AWS services (like Amazon Q, Amazon Bedrock, and AWS Incident Manager) and incumbents like ServiceNow, Datadog (DDOG), a company I covered in the past, and New Relic. That’s a big advantage. Companies don’t have to remove or replace the tools they already use to start using PagerDuty. That means they can just connect it to their current systems, start getting alerts right away, and over time, use it to do more advanced things like automatically managing and resolving problems as they happen.

Within this platform, PagerDuty has started to deploy its AI suite: AI Scribe, Advance, and three new AI agents slated for release this quarter, tools that offer intelligent event triage, contextual alerting, and incident documentation. I think the goal is obvious: shift from reactive troubleshooting to predictive and ultimately autonomous operations. But PagerDuty isn’t quite there yet. The AI still relies on predefined triggers and static logic, which, while valuable, actually stop short of true adaptivity. That gap, between deterministic automation and AI-native reasoning, is precisely where the next frontier lies, and PagerDuty knows it’s not the only one trying to cross it.

That awareness partly explains the 2023 acquisition of Jeli, a company focused on post-incident analysis and root cause exploration. Jeli enhances PagerDuty's platform with richer timelines, human-factor mapping, and collaborative retrospectives, functionality that matters deeply to regulated industries where auditability and procedural rigor are not optional. It also hints at PagerDuty’s evolving identity as a continuous improvement engine.

On the go-to-market side, the company is finally shaking off the last traces of adolescence. More than 60% of its enterprise sales reps are now past the one-year tenure mark, a milestone that signals increased consistency in pipeline execution. The company is also finalizing the appointment of a new Chief Revenue Officer, and has already revamped its sales coverage model. It’s also leaning into the public sector, where it now serves over 700 government organizations, and has secured FedRAMP Low authorization, with Moderate on the near-term horizon. So all of these steps PagerDuty is taking, like improving its sales team, expanding into government work, and getting important security approvals, help strengthen its position in the market. And they make it harder for other companies to take its customers just by adding new features quickly.

Recognition has followed.

PagerDuty recently earned Leader and Outperformer status on the GigaOm Radar, particularly within the “Innovation/Platform Play” quadrant. Still, it wouldn’t be a complete picture without acknowledging where the model strains. Pricing is one such tension. While the company offers clear, tiered plans like Professional, Business, and Enterprise, costs can scale sharply as customers add features, integrations, and automation. In other words, in a post-ZIRP world where CIOs are once again looking at the bottom line, that can become a sticking point. Likewise, PagerDuty’s low-code/no-code tools, though widely usable, don’t always offer the depth required by large-scale, custom-heavy enterprises.

Yet for any caveats we covered so far, PagerDuty’s balance sheet remains strong, with $597 million in cash and investments, giving it the runway and optionality to invest, acquire, or return capital. The company also raised its full-year EPS guidance to between $0.95 and $1.00, up from $0.90–$0.95, and increased its operating margin outlook to 20–21%.

PagerDuty’s Valuation

At first glance, the valuation doesn’t indicate any immediate red flags with its blended price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 18.18x and a forecasted 2025 P/E of 18.94x, the multiples seem modest, especially for a software name.

You could argue, in isolation, they might even suggest underappreciation. But once you overlay those figures onto PagerDuty’s weak earnings growth, inconsistent profitability, and decelerating revenue momentum, that argument begins to crack because these P/E ratios are trailing down a slope, with projections for 2026 at 17.24x, 2027 at 15.07x, and finally bottoming at 13.04x by 2028. What this outlines is a slow, gravity-bound correction.

And yet, the company’s Fair Value Ratio stands at 266.18x. While the modeled forward fair value of $14.11 is not dramatically below the market’s $16.10, I'd say that the relative closeness obscures the magnitude of the underlying distortion. This is an instance where the Fair Value Ratio this high is implying overvaluation because of a sustained and structural mispricing, one where the market seems to be pricing in long-range optimism despite tangible underperformance. Case in point, this disconnect is reinforced by the forward projection of a $20.95 share price by 2028, implying an annualized return of 10.34%. But those estimates are backfilled with assumptions about earnings growth and operational leverage that has not, to date, meaningfully shown up.

This wouldn't be so bad if the stock’s historical performance provided a counterweight. But it doesn’t. PagerDuty has posted a return of -17.15% over the past twelve months, while the S&P 500 (SP500) and the broader software index moved higher. And, you can see for yourself, that that gap isn’t exactly a one-off anomaly. PD has underperformed across every historical time frame, raising the fundamental question: why does it still command a valuation that implies future outperformance?

Yet, all is not lost. When we shift over to the view through the lens of operational execution, on that front, PagerDuty has been building a stronger case. Levered free cash flow increased 38.91% year-over-year, and looking forward, it’s expected to grow by 26.25%. Operating cash flow was even more impressive, jumping 63.8% YoY, with a 25.32% forward growth rate anticipated.

EBITDA growth is forecasted at 19.09%, while EBIT is expected to rise 26.10%. In other words, PagerDuty is keeping more of the money it makes from sales instead of spending it all, which means it's becoming more efficient and careful with its costs.

PagerDuty’s Risks & Headwinds

But even here, we run into some caveats. Despite that cash generation, the company doesn't seem to be able to translate it into consistent net income or strong EPS. The forward EPS growth is a tepid 13.02%, earning a C+ grade, while the long-term EPS compound annual growth rate (CAGR) sits at 11.24%, meriting a flat C, both below the sector median of 13.62%. The implication is hard to avoid: PagerDuty is executing better internally, but that execution hasn’t translated into bottom line leverage at the level expected by the market.

PagerDuty used to grow pretty fast. Looking at over the last five years, it was increasing its sales by about 25% each year, which showed the business was expanding steadily. But now, that growth has slowed way down. Over the past year, sales only grew by about 8.5%, and they’re expected to grow just 8.3% going forward. That’s a big slowdown, and it clearly shows the company isn’t growing like it used to. Keep in mind, the company’s own guidance is dialing that down even further: full-year fiscal 2026 revenue is now projected at $493–$499 million, down from the prior $500–$507 million, implying a 5–7% YoY increase. Q2 revenue growth is expected at just 6–7%, with the company anticipating it will exit the year at an even slower 5%. For a SaaS platform, you could say that this is erosion.

Where is that erosion coming from? Some of it is macro: customers consolidating vendors, delaying multiyear commitments, or trimming budgets due to uncertainty and AI volatility. But just as much of the pain is self-inflicted. The dollar-based net retention rate sits at 104%, signaling only modest expansion, and that's a disappointment for a recurring-revenue business. PagerDuty cited unexpected churn in the commercial segment and enterprise customer downgrades, some driven by M&A activity or layoffs. Even more directly, the company admitted it messed up internally. At the start of the quarter, it didn’t have enough salespeople in place or ready to go, which caused it to miss out on chances to make sales and slowed down how quickly things got moving.

To fix this, the company said that it's undertaking a wholesale reinvention of its enterprise sales motion like abandoning transactional tactics in favor of a value-based, strategic model. And while that's conceptually sound, the execution is far from complete. Many reps are still onboarding, productivity is uneven, and critically, the Chief Revenue Officer role is still vacant. The company is banking on a second-half turnaround in bookings and ARR, but the absence of a CRO and the ongoing transition suggest that any real impact won’t be felt until fiscal 2026, at best.

And even if sales execution improves, PagerDuty faces a widening field of competitors. The company’s traditional stronghold, incident response and automation, is now a crowded space, challenged by both entrenched hyperscalers and agile AI startups. To respond, PagerDuty has rolled out new products like Advance and AI Scribe. Early reviews are positive, but rollout is still in the early innings, and the tools require significant customer onboarding and integration support before they can be fully monetized. As of now, I'd say that they remain more potential than proof.

Final Takeaway

I’d rate PagerDuty a Hold. The company is clearly improving operationally by delivering strong margin expansion, elite free cash flow, and signs of platform maturity, but the stock’s structural overvaluation, slowing growth trajectory, and execution risks (especially in sales) counterbalance the progress. I think that until those improvements translate into consistent bottom line growth and reaccelerated ARR, I wouldn’t move higher than neutral.

data.title: Alibaba: Too Much Uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings of Alibaba Group Holding (NYSE:BABA) rose in the fourth quarter, despite considerable trade uncertainty in the market with respect to the profit prospects of ecommerce companies.

Alibaba Group Holdings’ profits also surged, by 22% YoY on an adjusted basis, thanks to robust growth in the company’s domestic ecommerce retail and wholesale businesses.

Though Taobao and Tmall, Alibaba Group Holdings’ biggest segment, produced solid growth in Q4'25, short-term uncertainty in regard to future tariffs could reflect profit headwinds for the ecommerce company.

Clearly, Alibaba Group Holdings did better than I anticipated in the last quarter of its 2025 financial year, but I think that shifting trade U.S. policy of the Trump Administration will make it harder (or at least more expensive) for Chinese companies to import products into the U.S.

Thus, I am not yet ready to modify my stock classification which last time was ‘Sell’.

Alibaba Group Holdings Reported Solid March-Quarter Results Despite Trade War

In the fourth quarter, Alibaba Group Holdings’ total sales rose 7% YoY to RMB236,454 million, which is roughly the equivalent of $32.6 billion.

Alibaba Group Holdings’ domestic commerce segment, which includes sales made in retail and wholesale markets, is what helped the ecommerce behemoth drive home good results in the March quarter, despite headwinds to the ecommerce industry in general. Alibaba Group Holdings’ commerce wholesale segment did particularly well, with sales growth of 17% YoY.

Interestingly enough, even sales in the Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group posted a 22% sales jump YoY and total sales of RMB33,579 million ($4.6 billion). The segment did not turn a profit, however, as opposed to Taobao and Tmall which is Alibaba Group Holdings’ profit driver.

The cause behind Alibaba Group Holdings’ robust results for the March quarter can be explained by looking at the retail spending trend for the Chinese economy.

While the market has been obsessed with the impact of the U.S.’s new tariffs on Chinese exports, Chinese consumers seem to be in good spirits and have proven to be a force to reckon with: China’s retail spending rose at its fastest pace in a year in March 2025, at a 5.9% annualized rate. In April, retail sales didn’t grow just as fast, but were still up a respectable 5.1%.

With domestic consumers opening up their wallets, and driving consumer spending, ecommerce companies whose operations are tilted toward domestic purchases, like Alibaba Group Holdings, did still pretty well, notwithstanding the considerable distress that has been playing out in markets since the U.S. announced a substantial shift in trade policy during the March quarter.

Alibaba Group Holdings’ Operating Profit Up-Scaling

Alibaba Group Holdings is showcasing consistent profitability, which has been mainly driven by its sizable retail and wholesale ecommerce in China. Consequently, Alibaba Group Holdings was not much impacted by the announcement of retaliatory tariffs last quarter and the ecommerce company produced a whopping RMB 28,465 million in operating profits in the March quarter, which equates to roughly $3.9 billion.

On a twelve-month basis, Alibaba Group Holdings’ operating profits rose an impressive 24% YoY to RMB140,905 million ($19.4 billion), thanks in large part because of the solid performance of its Taobao and Tmall segment.

As I pointed out in the previous section, robust ecommerce results were driven primarily because Chinese consumers came through, lifting China’s retail sales spending up at the fastest pace in a year.

And here are the trends for Alibaba Group Holdings’ operating profits as well as adjusted EBITDA, the two most important KPIs for the ecommerce company as far as I am concerned:

Taobao and Tmall are Alibaba Group Holdings’ profit machine and cash cow, accounting for almost all of the conglomerate’s profits. In Q4'25, Alibaba Group Holdings produced RMB 41,749 million ($5.8 billion), up 8% YoY and, as evident in the table below, the only segment with any real kind of profitability. Total operating profits skyrocketed a whopping 93% YoY and highlight Alibaba Group Holdings’ resilience even in a very challenging market.

Alibaba Group Holdings Is Not Yet A Buy

In my previous piece on Alibaba Group Holdings, I warned of potentially excessively bullish investor sentiment with regard to the Chinese ecommerce company and recommended taking profits at a price close to $146 per share.

The stock price of Alibaba Group Holdings is not overpriced right now, in my view, but also not a pressing buy taking into account how uncertain the present situation around profit trajectories is for ecommerce companies.

Alibaba Group Holdings is selling for a 2026e profit multiple of 11.0x, which is low compared to a peer group average of 20.7x.

China’s ecommerce companies in general are selling for discounted profit multiples amid persistent uncertainty about their profit prospects in the near term. My intrinsic value calculation in my last piece, which is when I modified my stock classification to ‘Sell’ was $118, which I continue to think is a reasonable valuation multiple.

That being said, the ecommerce peer group average profit multiple for 2026 is influenced primarily by two companies: MercadoLibre Inc. (MELI) as well as Coupang Inc. (CPNG) which sell for substantially above average Price-To-Earnings ratios of 36.5x and 40.6x.

I think that investors presently are not confronted with an attractive buying situation, unless trade uncertainty has been completely resolved.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be More Bearish, Creating Downside Risks For Investors

I am of the opinion that the U.S. government is seeking to fundamentally change its trade relationship with China, which makes it probable that negotiations, and potential rounds of escalations, will drag on for a while.

Presently, both countries have cut their retaliatory tariffs down to 10%, but I would not be surprised in the slightest if negotiations broke down and tariffs reset to their nominal amounts of 145%. Thus, ongoing trade uncertainty is probably the single most serious headwind for Alibaba Group Holdings.

On the flip side, a fast resolution of present grievances might result in an upward catalyst for the stock of Alibaba Group Holdings.

My Takeaway

Alibaba Group Holdings’ results for Q4'25 were very robust with respect to both the domestic results (Taobao and Tmall) as well as the international ecommerce results, as represented by the Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group.

Alibaba Group Holdings also exhibited a bullish operating profit trend, which has been underpinned by domestic increases in retail spending and, consequently, robust results for the company’s core ecommerce business, in both retail and wholesale segments.

Despite considerable order and trade uncertainty, Alibaba Group Holdings posted 7% sales growth YoY, while its operating profits skyrocketed at an even faster rate of 93%. Despite this growth, an uncertain trade outlook clouds the profit prospects for Alibaba Group Holdings, and the stock is also selling at my intrinsic value estimate of $118 per share.

Taking into consideration that the U.S. and China have not yet reached an agreement about tariffs, investors should not take a position in Alibaba Group Holdings right now.

I think investors should wait with a potential purchase until they have some clarity as to how the U.S.-Sino relationship will be structured moving forward.

data.title: Affiliated Managers Group Q1: Declines In All Key Metrics
data.description:
I rate Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. (NYSE:AMG) a Hold, for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. AMG provides investment management services to clients that manage money or asset managers. As the clients do well, AMG does well. Over the past 10 years, AMG has underperformed the financial services sector. However, in the more recent 5 years, the company has outperformed. AMG touts its strategy, which could account for the change in trajectory; however, the Q1 earnings results were poor, creating doubts that the company will continue to outperform. The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock area:

  • Last 5-year total return outperformed the financial services sector
  • $712B assets under management (AUM)
  • Focused on share buybacks vs. dividends to return capital to shareholders
  • Pays a tiny dividend of $0.01 per share which raises the question – why bother?

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold at 3.12 (as of 5/29/25), which supports my rating. Note the “D” rating for Valuation.

Investment Management Services

Headquartered in West Palm Beach, Florida, Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. operates as an investment management company providing investment management services to mutual funds, institutional clients, retailers, and high net worth individuals in the United States.

It provides advisory or sub-advisory services to mutual funds. These funds are distributed to retail, high net worth, and institutional clients directly and through intermediaries, including independent investment advisors, retirement plan sponsors, broker-dealers, major fund marketplaces, and bank trust departments.

Affiliated Managers Group was formed in 1993 with additional offices in Prides Crossing, Massachusetts; Stamford, Connecticut; London, United Kingdom; Dubai, United Arab Emirates; Sydney, Australia; Hong Kong; Tokyo, Japan, Zurich, Switzerland, and Delaware.

From the investor presentation, here are some of the clients they service in the alternative asset class:

Of the clients listed above, I'm most familiar with Yacktman Funds, Tweedy, Browne, and NorthBridge.

Q1 2025 Earnings Results – Terrible

On May 8, 2025, the company declared Q1 results, which were not good. The chart below tells the story.

While AUM grew from $699.4 to $712.2 billion, just about every other important metric was down appreciably. For example, Aggregate fees dropped –13.7% to $1.27 billion. Net income dropped -51% to $72.4 million. Earnings per share dropped –46.9% to $2.20.

These results were so bad that the company didn't include percentage change in the charts, perhaps to hide the fact. I can imagine the CEO had a tough time coming up with a quote to make it sound positive.

The few positive actions included a new partnership with Verition Fund Management and Qualitas Energy, together with Q1 investment in NorthBridge Partners, further diversify AMG’s business and broaden its participation in alternatives, in line with its growth strategy, and the company repurchased approximately $173 million in common stock in the first quarter.

Performance To Date

Normally, I look at a 10-year performance. In AMG’s case, it was so bad I thought I had made a mistake, so I redid the data. Here is what I saw. An investment of $10,000 in AMG over 10 years would have lost money, ending up with $7,986. This is in stark comparison to the financial sector (XLF) overall, which tripled to $30,460 despite going through various crisis moments such as Covid. Based on this chart, I was considering a “Strong Sell” recommendation. It is still hard to ignore once you've seen, it because it creates doubt about the company’s ability to succeed.

I ran the same chart but for 5 years. So, an investment of $10,000 in AMG held over 5 years grew to $25,290 which indeed outperformed the financial sector, as XLF ended up just short at $23,450. Sometimes companies have a hard time getting started, or they are going through a transition. In this case, the stock has definitely seen better performance in more recent years. Will it continue? Based on Q1 results, I'm not too sure. Either way, this company is not a consistent, reliable path to wealth.

Valuation

AMG pays a $0.01 quarterly dividend, with an annual yield of 0.02%. It has a 5-year growth rate showing a decline of -47.15%. Early in the company’s history, it paid a dividend payout as high as $1.28 per year, but starting in 2021, the company cut the dividend dramatically to one cent per share per quarter. It seems pointless to keep it at such a low level, in my view.

In the Investor Presentation, AMG showed how the Adjusted EBITDA grew at a CAGR of 5% from 2020 to 2024. Similarly, the economic earnings per share grew at a CAGR of 13%. While positive growth is good, these are not the expected growth rates from a company that is growing at a rapid pace. These are the kinds of charts to show when you can manipulate the statistics to make your story. 4 years is a short time, and these growth rates don't indicate a high-growth, high-performing company, in my view.

AMG’s peers are included in the chart below, ranked by market capitalization. Within the peer group, AMG stands out for having a "non-dividend" vs. an average of 4-5%. AMG does post the second-highest 5-year total return at 157% and a respectable return on equity of 13.7%.

Invesco Ltd. (IVZ) pays the second-highest dividend yield of 5.8% with a solid 5-year total return, and the worst ROE in the group at 5.1%.

Janus Henderson Group plc (JHG) pays a 4% dividend, has a 5-year total return of 118%, and a weak ROE.

AllianceBernstein Holding (AB) pays the highest dividend at 8.1%, with a strong 5-year total return and good ROE of 21%.

Victory Capital Holdings Inc. (VCTR) stands out for its super-high 5-year total return of 332.1% and 83% dividend growth rate. It's one of the asset managers to watch in this space, showing extraordinary growth. They manage USAA insurance.

Cohen & Steers, Inc. (CNS), one of my personal favorite asset managers due to their closed-end fund offerings, pays a 3.2% dividend and has the highest ROE of 31%.

In comparing the peers based on total return, below is the 5-year chart. Normally, I would use 10 years, but the chart gets strange as each firm has had various crises or opportunities which skew the data. Victory Capital outperforms, while AMG comes in second place.

Outlook

AMG is heavily reliant on client success in the investing environment. Realizing the U.S. is in a volatile period, it is still considered highly favorable to business and investment. Therefore, it is expected that investment-based businesses will tend to grow within the next few years with lower taxes on businesses and deregulation. For some industries, such as cryptocurrency, it is considered a boom time as the President favors the sector.

AMG identifies its strategy to drive growth and compound earnings with the following 4 pillars: Affiliate Performance, Organic Growth, Affiliate Investments, and Share Repurchases. In essence, they succeed when their clients grow. The company will also buy back shares at the right time, which is a good way to build share value, in my view. Clearly, they are less interested in the dividend.

Risks To Rating

The largest risk to AMG is macroenvironmental volatility that causes investors to pull back on investments, preferring to stay in cash. Lower market values decrease fee income on AUM. The recent tariff war has created volatility in trading, sending shares up and down like waves on an ocean. As time goes on, we will see the effect of tariffs on company earnings, which may put pressure on prices as earnings are impacted.

The second-largest risk is competition, in my view. AMG is competing in a highly competitive field, trying to protect its servicing niche.

Bottom Line

I rate Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. a Hold for capital appreciation-focused investors looking for growing companies in the financial services sector. AMG just posted miserable Q1 results, which doesn't create a great deal of faith in management or their strategy, in my view.

The company provides investment management services to clients and invests in certain sectors such as alternatives and long equities. In the last 5 years, the performance has exceeded that of the financial services sector, however looking at a longer period such as 10 years, they are dramatically worse.

The company pays a tiny dividend of $0.01 per share every quarter but spends a great deal of its capital on buying back shares. It currently manages $712 billion AUM. Going forward, it's unclear as to the direction of the stock; therefore, I recommend holding at this time.

data.title: Weitz Multi Cap Equity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Against a challenging market backdrop, we were pleased to report “black ink” for the first quarter of 2025. The Multi Cap Equity Fund’s Institutional Class returned +0.81% for the quarter, comparing favorably to the Russell 3000 Index’s loss of -4.72%. The “AI Trade” was dealt a setback in late January after Chinese firm DeepSeek announced its advanced AI model had been developed at a fraction of the cost of U.S. competitors. This led to greater investor scrutiny of U.S. firms’ spending plans.

In the quarter’s closing weeks, investors began bracing for the new administration’s trade and tariff plans, set to be announced in the early days of April. Uncertainties such as these (particularly of this magnitude) challenge investors’ and business leaders’ confidence, resulting in a pullback from equities heading into quarter end.

Notable 1Q Contributors

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), the Fund’s largest holding, often benefits in volatile markets. Berkshire’s operations include a wide swath of American and global businesses that are surely impacted by changes in trade policy. However, Berkshire’s “fortress” balance sheet (including over $300 billion of cash) and strong record of capital allocation suggest the company is poised to benefit from broader market turbulence.

Aerospace parts and services provider HEICO (HEI.A) delivered standout earnings, featuring stronger-than-expected growth in both of its segments. HEICO’s value proposition of saving customers money by adopting their cost-effective, alternative parts remains as relevant as ever, and we believe it will drive growth into the future. Visa (V) similarly delivered solid growth despite cross-border volumes continuing to lag historical trends. Our Charter-related holdings (including Liberty Broadband (LBRDA)) results are better described as “good enough” relative to low expectations. Capital investments for charter should peak this year with improving free cash flow characteristics on the horizon. Lastly, investors rewarded LKQ (LKQ) management’s focus on improving margins, reducing debt, and returning cash to shareholders

Notable 1Q Detractors

On the negative side of the ledger, Alphabet (GOOG) was the Fund’s top detractor. The company’s Google unit is on the front lines of the “Traditional Search vs. GenAI” battle. With its own GenAI capabilities embedded in Google Search results, it’s helpful that Alphabet has a foot in each camp. Nevertheless, investors fear a significant shift of search share in favor of competitors, specifically ChatGPT. We believe the debate is far from settled and has overshadowed other strong businesses inside Alphabet (YouTube, to name one). We elected to reduce our position size early in the quarter to reflect the heightened competitive pressures.

Perimeter Solutions (PRM) reported a strong fourth quarter, capping an above-average fire season, yet shares were down in 1Q. Investors are likely anticipating a reduced wildfire experience in 2025 and are expressing some skepticism over the announced, modest-sized acquisition of IMS Corp, a maker of niche printed circuit boards. While far afield of Perimeter’s core business, management has long articulated a “platform” approach to acquisitions, pursuing different business lines that meet their criteria and provide additional verticals for organic growth and bolt-on acquisitions.

The demand environment for life sciences remains in flux as cuts to federal health agencies are underway and research grant funding is under question. In response, investors have shed shares of industry “pure plays” (Bio-Techne (TECH), Danaher (DHR)) and those with more modest life science exposure (industrial machinery company IDEX Corp. (IEX)) alike. Near-term uncertainty remains high, but we remain confident in the long-term opportunity and added to Danaher and Bio-Techne this quarter.

1Q Portfolio Changes

Portfolio activity was elevated in the first quarter. In addition to our life sciences additions, we initiated new positions in three companies. U.S. broadband giant Comcast (CMCSA) and leading consulting and IT services firm Accenture (ACN) returned to the portfolio after multi-year hiatuses following underwhelming earnings reports that created compelling entry points. We also purchased shares in IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX), the leading provider of diagnostic lab equipment for veterinary practices. Growth in testing volumes has slowed as the wave of pet adoptions during COVID moves into a lower utilization phase of life.

In the longer term, however, several structural tailwinds remain in place including growing pet ownership, increased adoption of diagnostic tools and underpenetrated international markets. Funding these purchases were trims of Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet, and LICT Corp. (OTCPK:LICT). We also exited our position in Veralto (VLTO), a profitable spin-off of Danaher, and completed our sale of Liberty Global (LBTYA) shares.
